bibliographic_data.title: Accessibility Across Borders
bibliographic_data.abstract: Postcolonial computing emphasizes that the design, development, and use of technology varies within different cultures, as well as the need for understanding these design practices and what implications there are in a global context. Within human-computer interaction (HCI), there is also a concept called WEIRD (Western, Educated, Industrialized, Rich, and Democratic), which highlights a requirement to better understand how current HCI principles fit in other countries and it emphasizes the necessity to more critically review our processes. A CHI 2021 paper has identified that 73% of nearly 3,300 papers from 2016 to 2020 included Western participants and, in general, fit the WEIRD profile, calling attention to the need for more international focus in HCI research studies. Since prior work has identified that cultural differences influence user design preferences and interaction methods, as well as emphasizing the need to reflect on the appropriateness of popular HCI principles, we believe that it is equally important to apply this inquiry to digital accessibility and how accessibility fits within the design process around the world.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper investigates how digital designers in different parts of the world consider accessibility and whether current accessibility resources meet or conflict with their approach to design, focusing on Iranian web and mobile designers.
core_content.key_methodology: A contextual design approach using established qualitative methods and contextual inquiry will be used to understand the current accessibility support available to Iranian web and mobile designers. The study will also be conducted remotely due to COVID-19 (e.g., online questionnaires, online semi-structured interviews, remote diary studies).
outcomes: Identify how best to implement new accessibility guidelines and design tool features that meet the needs of Iranian designers.
Understand the current accessibility support available to Iranian web and mobile designers.
core_content.full_text_markdown: The paper emphasizes the need for a more international focus in human-computer interaction (HCI) research, particularly regarding digital accessibility. It highlights how cultural differences can influence user design preferences and interaction methods, and suggests that popular HCI principles may not be universally applicable. The authors propose to investigate how digital designers in different parts of the world consider accessibility, focusing on Iranian web and mobile designers due to their recent history for establishing disability rights, distinct linguistic differences, and lack of accessible resources. They plan to use a contextual design approach with qualitative methods such as online questionnaires and interviews to understand current practices and identify ways to improve accessibility guidelines and tools.
